chunk_summary: This section outlines bedside diagnostic criteria and anthropometric measurements for identifying severe acute malnutrition (SAM) in children aged 6–59 months, emphasizing MUAC, WHZ/WFH, and oedema markers for diagnosis and management pathways.
learning_objectives: Understand bedside diagnostic criteria for SAM, including MUAC, WHZ/WFH, and oedema markers.
Learn how to minimize anthropometric measurement errors during SAM diagnosis.
Recognize the importance of tracking clinical status and weight changes during stabilization and catch-up feeding.
Differentiate diagnostic thresholds for infants under 6 months versus children aged 6–59 months.
key_facts: SAM diagnosis criteria: MUAC <115 mm, WHZ/WFH <-3, or bilateral pitting nutritional oedema.
MUAC and WHZ identify overlapping but distinct high-risk groups; both should be documented at baseline.
Weight is essential for prescribing therapeutic food quantities, but oedema can distort early weight changes.
Anthropometric measurements require calibrated tools and proper technique to minimize errors.
Infants under 6 months require a separate feeding-effectiveness pathway rather than standard thresholds.
reference_quote: Indian guidance from MoHFW and IAP uses these same markers and treats them as pathway-defining rather than merely descriptive, because phenotype and complications determine disposition, fluid strategy, and feeding volumes.
document_key_topics: Special considerations for infants under 6 months
